claim: Lord et al. 2007 (JAVMA 230:211-216) found that 132 of 187 lost dogs (71%) were recovered, with a median time to recovery of 2 days (range 0.5–21 days); recovery was primarily through animal agency contact (34.8%), dog license tag (18.2%), and posted neighborhood signs (15.2%).
stance_target: temporal recovery window — validates early-phase (days 1-3) as highest-yield search window
scope_conditions: 187 lost dogs, Montgomery County Ohio, June-September 2005; telephone survey; 71% recovery rate
quoted_support: 132 of the 187 (71%) dogs were recovered; median time to recovery was 2 days (range, 0.5 to 21 days). Dogs were recovered primarily through a call or visit to an animal agency (46 [34.8%]), a dog license tag (24 [18.2%]), and posting of neighborhood signs (20 [15.2%]).

claim: Lord 2007 established that owners with identification on their dog (ID tag, dog license, rabies tag, or microchip) had higher recovery likelihood; 48% of lost dogs had some identification at the time of loss.
stance_target: identification as a recovery factor — relevant to intake triage priority
scope_conditions: 187 lost dogs, Ohio, 2005; hazard ratio analysis
quoted_support: Eighty-nine (48%) dogs had some type of identification at the time they were lost. Owners had a higher likelihood of recovery when they called an animal agency (hazard ratio, 2.1), visited an animal agency (1.8), and posted neighborhood signs.

claim: NOTE — full text of Lord 2007 JAVMA is paywalled (403 on AVMA journals); data reported here is from web-search synthesis of published abstracts and secondary citations, not directly from the primary PDF.
stance_target: source quality flag for Lord 2007 data
quoted_support: N/A — data from abstract-level web search synthesis.
